results.possibleIdentity: Adrian B. Ettlinger (1925–2013) was a prominent American electrical engineer and software developer who spent much of his career at the Columbia Broadcasting System (CBS). Known as a visionary in the field of television technology, he is often cited as the "engineering father" of non-linear video editing. His key technical achievements include leading the development of the first computerized on-air television switching control system, conceptualizing the first practical stop-action instant replay system (for which he received an Emmy in 1966), and conceiving the CMX 600, the first computer-controlled non-linear video editing system. He also developed an advanced computer-controlled theater lighting system used in major theatrical and television installations. Ettlinger was an active collaborator in the intersection of technology and the arts; he was associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that paired artists with engineers. His E.A.T. index card identified him as a CBS Television employee based in Hastings-on-Hudson, New York, with expertise in television and computers. He was awarded the David Sarnoff Medal by the Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers (SMPTE) in 1976 for his pioneering work. In his later years, he became known for developing popular software, including the FreeCell Pro solitaire solver and the AniMap historical atlas program.
